text: GPU industry semiconductor supply chain research

# GPU Semiconductor Supply Chain — Research Brief

*No prior memory/branch context existed on this topic; brief is built from fresh web evidence (late 2025 / Q1 2026).*

---

## 1. Supply Chain Structure at a Glance

A frontier AI GPU (e.g., NVIDIA B200, AMD MI355) is the composite output of **three tightly coupled chokepoints**, each with different players, economics, and lead times:

| Layer | Dominant Player(s) | Cost Share of Finished Chip | Supply Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Advanced logic die (3nm/2nm) | TSMC (~90% share of leading-edge) | ~40–50% | Critically constrained |
| HBM3e / HBM4 memory | SK Hynix (~50%), Samsung (~30%), Micron (~20%) | ~30–35% | Fully allocated through 2026 |
| CoWoS advanced packaging | TSMC (primary), OSATs: Amkor, SPIL, ASE | ~5–7% | Severely constrained — the binding bottleneck |
| Substrates/PCBs | Ibiden, Unimicron | ~4–6% | Constrained |
| Power/other ICs + Final assembly | Various | ~5–9% | Stable |

Source: [Silicon Analysts, Jan 2026](https://siliconanalysts.com/analysis/ai-chip-demand-surge-tsmc-3nm-supply-strained-lead-times-exceed-50-weeks)

---

## 2. The Three Bottlenecks

### A. Wafer fabrication (TSMC N3/N2)
**Evidence:**
- TSMC 3nm fully allocated through late 2027; lead times **>50 weeks**, up from ~30 weeks six months earlier. Wafer price ~**$17k–$22k** ([Silicon Analysts](https://siliconanalysts.com/analysis/ai-chip-demand-surge-tsmc-3nm-supply-strained-lead-times-exceed-50-weeks)).
- 2nm (N2/A16) fully booked; wafer costs expected >$30k. Apple holds ~50%+ of N2 2026–2027 allocation ([Silicon Analysts, Q1 2026](https://siliconanalysts.com/analysis/foundry-allocation-status-q1-2026)).
- After Jensen Huang's Taiwan trip, TSMC added ~20k WPM of 3nm capacity to serve NVIDIA's **Rubin** platform; pushing 2026 CapEx to **$48–56B** vs. $40.9B in 2025 ([Longbridge/Morgan Stanley](https://longbridge.com/en/news/269358704)).

### B. CoWoS advanced packaging — *the binding constraint*
**Evidence:**
- TSMC monthly CoWoS capacity: **~75–80k WPM (end-2025) → ~120–130k WPM (end-2026)**, a 58% YoY expansion. Non-TSMC OSAT capacity rising from 26k to 40k wafers/yr ([Digitimes/Longbridge](https://longbridge.com/en/news/269358704)).
- **NVIDIA alone reserved ~800k–850k wafers of CoWoS for 2026** — >50% of total; Broadcom ~240k (for Meta/Google TPU), AMD third (~150k for MI400/MI450), MediaTek ~20k for ASIC customers ([Global Semi Research, Dec 2025](https://globalsemiresearch.substack.com/p/tsmcs-cowos-capacity-scaling-up-outsourcing); [Wccftech](https://wccftech.com/nvidia-alone-has-tsmc-advanced-packaging-lines-booked-for-several-years-ahead)).
- All three TSMC CoWoS back-end fabs (AP3, AP5, AP6) **fully booked, 52–78 week lead times**.
- TSMC outsourcing ~240k–270k wafers/yr — **Amkor 180–190k**, SPIL 60–80k. OSAT margins: substrate-only 35–40%; full CoW+OS 40–45%; CoWoS-R up to 50%.
- [CNBC/TSMC interview, Apr 2026](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/08/tsmc-nvidia-advanced-packaging-intel.html): TSMC confirms NVIDIA has reserved a "majority" of the most advanced packaging; ASE projects advanced packaging sales to double in 2026.
- Transition roadmap: **CoWoS-S → CoWoS-L (silicon bridges + RDL, ~3–4x value per unit) → CoPoS** (Chip-on-Panel-on-Substrate, volume production targeted late 2027).

### C. HBM memory — tight oligopoly
**Evidence:**
- Micron's CBO stated in Aug 2025 that **all 2026 HBM output is sold out** ([Digitimes](https://www.digitimes.com/news/a20250818PD207/micron-2026-hbm-sk-hynix-samsung.html)).
- HBM3e (8-hi and 12-hi) **fully allocated through 2026**; prices +15–22% YoY.
- SK Hynix ramped the industry's first HBM4 mass production in Feb 2026 at M16 (Icheon); M15X adds 50k WPM by 2027 ([Seoul Economic Daily](https://en.sedaily.com/finance/2025/12/26/sk-hynix-leads-hbm4-race-with-worlds-first-mass-production)).
- Forecast 2026 HBM market share: SK Hynix ~50%, Samsung ~30%, Micron ~20%.
- **NVIDIA's HBM mix shifts** in 2026: SK Hynix share drops from ~90% of NVIDIA's HBM today to ~50% as Samsung (post-certification) and Micron take share ([Digitimes](https://digitimes.com/news/a20250820PD240/hbm-nvidia-2026-samsung-sk-hynix.html)).
- TAM: Micron TAM guidance $18B (2024) → $35B (2025); ~$100B envisaged by HBM4E generation (~2028).

---

## 3. Demand Side — Concentration in 4 Buyers

Per [Epoch AI (Mar 2026)](http://www.epoch.ai/data-insights/ai-chip-supply-chain-constraints): NVIDIA + Google + AMD + Amazon consumed **~90% of global CoWoS and HBM by value in 2025**, but only **~12% of advanced-node logic wafers**. This asymmetry is why packaging and memory — not wafers — are the true scaling limits.

- **NVIDIA**: >50% of CoWoS, 60–70% of CoWoS-L for Blackwell/Rubin. 2025 CoWoS: ~400k wafers → 2026: ~700k wafers (+75%).
- **Google TPU**: Demand-side request ~240k wafers in 2026 (v7 on CoWoS-S, early v8 CoWoS-L), but constrained delivery of ~150–180k → only ~3.2M TPUs vs. ~6M target. Reliant on Amkor partnership.
- **AMD**: ~150k CoWoS-L wafers in 2026 for MI400/MI450.
- **Broadcom**: ~240k wafers, primarily Meta & Google TPU ASIC backends.
- **Custom silicon (AWS Trainium, Microsoft Maia, Meta MTIA)** projected to reach **~45% of CoWoS-based accelerator shipments by 2026**, up from 20–30% in 2024 ([NextWaves Insight](https://nextwavesinsight.com/ai-semiconductor-supply-chain-tsmc-capacity-2026)).

---

## 4. Geopolitics & Export Controls

- **April 2025**: U.S. restricts even NVIDIA's China-tuned **H20** chip; $5.5B inventory charge ([NPR](https://www.npr.org/2025/04/16/nx-s1-5366665/nvidia-china-h20-chips-exports)). Same license requirement extended to AMD MI308X, Intel Gaudi.
- **Jul 2025**: License regime eased; H20 and a new Blackwell-inspired RTX PRO variant approved for China ([DCD](https://www.datacenterdynamics.com/en/news/us-agrees-to-grant-nvidia-h20-export-licenses-as-chipmaker-unveils-new-blackwell-inspired-gpu-for-china/)).
- **Aug 2025**: NVIDIA & AMD agree to **15% revenue share** to U.S. government on China chip sales; U.S. takes 10% stake in Intel.
- **Nov 2025 (Trump/60 Minutes)**: Top-end Blackwell reserved **U.S.-only**; only lesser variants eligible for China ([Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/world/china/trump-says-nvidias-blackwell-ai-chip-not-other-people-2025-11-03)).
- **Dec 2025**: **H200 cleared for China export** with **25% U.S. import tax** collected at the Taiwan→U.S. transshipment point, plus security review ([Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/world/china/us-open-up-exports-nvidia-h200-chips-china-semafor-reports-2025-12-08/); [CNN](https://www.cnn.com/2025/12/08/tech/nvidia-h200-chips-china-trump-export)). Similar framework being built for AMD/Intel.
- **CRS Report R48642**: Export-control stack now covers HBM, DRAM, advanced packaging SME, with "Red-Flag" due-diligence rules, and targets Huawei Ascend-tied entities.
- **Concentration risk**: Every TSMC Taiwan fab carries "elevated" geo-risk rating; all 12 fully-booked-or-severely-constrained fabs sit in Taiwan (10) or the U.S. (2). TSMC Arizona Fab 21 P1 is the only sub-5nm capacity on U.S. soil today.

---

## 5. Capacity Response

- **TSMC Arizona**: Fab 21 P1 (N4/N5) ramping; two packaging fabs planned for Arizona, mass production by 2028–2029. Today, 100% of TSMC packaging is still done in Taiwan, even for U.S.-fabbed wafers ([CNBC](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/08/tsmc-nvidia-advanced-packaging-intel.html)).
- **TSMC Chiayi**: Two new CoWoS plants ramping to MP in 2026–2027.
- **SK Hynix**: M15X clean room online May 2025; 50k WPM at full ramp in 2027.
- **Samsung**: 2nm GAA fabs (Hwaseong S3, Pyeongtaek P4, Taylor TX) in early access, volume late 2026–2027.
- **Intel**: EMIB/Foveros gaining traction as CoWoS alternative; Amazon and Google reportedly exploring it.
- **OSAT outsourcing**: Amkor and SPIL now qualified CoWoS nodes; ASE projects advanced packaging revenue to **double in 2026**.

---

## 6. Belief Update

**Evidence → takeaways:**
1. The bottleneck narrative has definitively **migrated from wafers to packaging + HBM**. This is now consensus among TSMC management, Morgan Stanley, Epoch AI, TrendForce, and multiple independent analysts.
2. **NVIDIA's structural advantage is secured**: it has effectively pre-booked the scarce resource (CoWoS-L) for 2026–2027, forcing competitors (AMD, custom ASICs) to fight for residual allocation.
3. **Hyperscaler custom silicon is a hedge, not a replacement**: TPU/Trainium/Maia/MTIA all draw on the same CoWoS + HBM pool. Google's ~40% CoWoS shortfall vs. target illustrates that owning the design doesn't solve the packaging constraint.
4. **Geopolitics have become an accounting line item**: the 15–25% U.S. revenue share on China sales is now an embedded supply-chain tax — not a binary on/off risk — though the top-end (Blackwell/Rubin) remains U.S.-only.
5. **2027–2028 is the re-rating moment**: CoPoS, HBM4E, Arizona packaging, and Samsung/Taylor 2nm all target this window. If expansion lands on schedule, structural tightness eases; if AI demand keeps compounding (as it has for 3 straight years), the constraint persists in a new form.

---

## 7. Conclusion & Signals to Watch

**Conclusion:** The GPU supply chain in 2025–2026 is a **3-dimensional constraint** (logic + packaging + memory), not a wafer-supply story. Scaling is gated by CoWoS above all, then HBM, with logic a softer (but tightening at N3/N2) constraint. NVIDIA has the strongest allocation position; hyperscalers are responding with ASICs but from within the same chokepoints; geopolitics has evolved from outright ban risk to structured revenue-share frameworks.

**High-signal data to monitor:**
- TSMC quarterly CoWoS WPM disclosure and CapEx revisions
- Memory vendors' Q2/Q3 earnings for HBM4 yield ramp and NVIDIA qualification status
- Amkor / SPIL / ASE advanced packaging revenue growth (proxy for OSAT absorption of overflow)
- Next U.S. Commerce Department rulemaking on HBM/packaging SME controls
- NVIDIA's Rubin launch timing and CoWoS-L mix
- Samsung 2nm yield closure vs. TSMC at equivalent node

**Top risks:**
- Taiwan concentration (all critically-constrained fabs sit there)
- Yield stumble at TSMC N2 or SK Hynix HBM4 would cascade across the entire AI roadmap
- Export-control reversals if U.S.–China tensions escalate beyond the current revenue-share equilibrium
- CoPoS transition missteps (2027) risking stranded CoWoS-S investment

---

### Sources
- Silicon Analysts — [3nm & CoWoS](https://siliconanalysts.com/analysis/ai-chip-demand-surge-tsmc-3nm-supply-strained-lead-times-exceed-50-weeks), [Foundry allocation Q1 2026](https://siliconanalysts.com/analysis/foundry-allocation-status-q1-2026)
- Global Semi Research — [TSMC CoWoS scaling & outsourcing](https://globalsemiresearch.substack.com/p/tsmcs-cowos-capacity-scaling-up-outsourcing)
- Epoch AI — [AI chip supply chain constraints](http://www.epoch.ai/data-insights/ai-chip-supply-chain-constraints)
- Longbridge / Morgan Stanley — [TSMC 127k WPM target](https://longbridge.com/en/news/269358704)
- CNBC — [TSMC North America packaging interview](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/08/tsmc-nvidia-advanced-packaging-intel.html)
- NextWaves Insight — [Shifting bottleneck](https://nextwavesinsight.com/ai-semiconductor-supply-chain-tsmc-capacity-2026)
- Digitimes — [Micron 2026 HBM sold out](https://www.digitimes.com/news/a20250818PD207/micron-2026-hbm-sk-hynix-samsung.html), [NVIDIA HBM reshuffle](https://digitimes.com/news/a20250820PD240/hbm-nvidia-2026-samsung-sk-hynix.html)
- Seoul Economic Daily — [SK Hynix HBM4 MP](https://en.sedaily.com/finance/2025/12/26/sk-hynix-leads-hbm4-race-with-worlds-first-mass-production)
- Reuters — [Trump Blackwell restriction](https://www.reuters.com/world/china/trump-says-nvidias-blackwell-ai-chip-not-other-people-2025-11-03), [H200 export deal](https://www.reuters.com/world/china/us-open-up-exports-nvidia-h200-chips-china-semafor-reports-2025-12-08/)
- NPR — [H20 controls](https://www.npr.org/2025/04/16/nx-s1-5366665/nvidia-china-h20-chips-exports)
- Congressional Research Service — [R48642 Export Controls](https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/R48642)

Want me to drill deeper into any one layer — e.g., HBM4 vendor economics, the CoPoS transition, TSMC Arizona ramp, or the China export-control framework's quarterly impact on NVIDIA revenue?
reasoning:
I've got solid material to work with, so I'll organize this into a structured brief that addresses the supply chain architecture, identifies the critical constraints like advanced packaging and memory production, maps out the major suppliers and manufacturers at each tier, and examines demand from the key players driving GPU adoption.

Now I'm rounding out the analysis by covering capacity roadmaps, the geopolitical landscape around chip exports, and the emerging risks worth monitoring.

results.summary: Micron has already booked all of its 2026 HBM production, signaling a market-tightness catalyst as rivals SK Hynix and Samsung work to catch up in the high-bandwidth memory race. This suggests tighter HBM supply in the near term and potential pricing/power dynamics as Nvidia and other AMD/AI workloads contend with constrained supply.

results.title: HBM roadmaps for Micron, Samsung, and SK hynix: To HBM4 and beyond | Tom's Hardware
results.author:
Features 
 
 
By
 Anton Shilov   
 
published
 
6 August 2025
highlights:
SK hynix has begun mass production of 36 GB 12-Hi HBM3E chips, whereas Micron has been sampling similar products since September, with mass production of the new packages understood to be imminent.
[...]
Samsung and SK hynix are rumored to introduce their first HBM4 offerings around Q3 2025, whereas Micron is projected to follow in Q4 2025. In both cases, 'introductions' likely mean the delivery of the first working samples to partners like AMD and Nvidia, not high-volume manufacturing.
[...]
Curiously, Micron began discussing HBM4E around a year before the planned mass production of HBM4 was set to commence. Typically, 'extended' variants of HBM specifications are introduced years after the original standard. According to Micron's official roadmap, HBM4E is set to arrive in late 2027.
[...]
HBM4E is likely to be used in the generation after the release of Nvidia's upcoming Rubin architecture and AMD's MI400 AI accelerator. Both are slated to support HBM4 in 2026.
results.summary: HBM roadmaps from Micron, Samsung, and SK hynix point to HBM4-era memory with increasing bandwidth and efficiency beyond current HBM3 implementations, signaling continued high-bandwidth memory expansion for AI, HPC, and datapath accelerators. The likely market catalyst is accelerated adoption of HBM4-enabled GPUs/accelerators to meet growing AI workload demands and bandwidth-saturated data centers.

results.summary: Samsung plans to launch an enhanced HBM3E in Q1 2025 and aims to double its HBM supply in 2025 to catch up with SK Hynix, signaling stronger memory capabilities and potential price/availability shifts in high-bandwidth memory markets. This move could act as a key catalyst for memory suppliers and AI/accelerator vendors reliant on HBM3E.

results.title: SK Hynix Leads HBM4 Race with World's First Mass Production in February - Seoul Economic Daily
results.author: Seoul Economic Daily
highlights:
SK hynix (000660.KS) will begin the world's first mass production of sixth-generation high bandwidth memory (HBM4) in February 2025 at its M16 facility in Icheon, Gyeonggi Province, gaining a critical head start over rivals Samsung Electronics and Micron.
[...]
SK hynix's M15X fab, which received investment of more than 20 trillion won, will begin operating its first clean room in May 2025, with mass production shipments starting in November. At full capacity in 2027, the facility will add 50,000 wafers per month.
[...]
Financial industry analysts forecast SK hynix will maintain over 50% HBM market share next year, while Samsung Electronics will hold around 30% and Micron approximately 20%. The company is expected to post operating profit of 15 trillion won in the fourth quarter, with full-year 2025 operating profit potentially reaching up to 93 trillion won—the highest in company history.
results.summary: SK hynix will become the first to mass-produce HBM4 in February 2025 at its M16 plant, leveraging a 12nm TSMC process to double bandwidth and cut power by over 40%, potentially capturing over 50% of the HBM market and widening its lead over Samsung and Micron. This timeline positions SK hynix as a key AI hardware supplier ahead of Nvidia’s Rubin chip cycle and could drive sizable near-term profitability.

results.title: SK hynix unveils 2025 shipment plans for most up-to-date HBM chip - The Korea Herald
results.author: Jie Ye-eun
highlights:
SK hynix CEO Kwak Noh-jung unveiled the company’s plan to start mass production of the industry's most up-to-date high-bandwidth memory chips early next year.
[...]
“We anticipated that the market for the 16-layer product will begin to open (the sixth-generation) HBM4,” he said in a keynote speech at SK AI Summit 2024 in Seoul on Monday. "We are developing the new product to ensure technical stability.”
[...]
SK hynix began supplying the industry’s first eight-layer HBM3E product to US chip giant Nvidia in March, and started mass production of the 12-layer HBM3E chip last month, aiming for shipments in the fourth quarter.
[...]
Following this, the company plans to supply the 16-layer HBM3E product in the first half of next year and introduce a 12-layer HBM4 chip in the latter half.
[...]
The company aims to further solidify its market leadership by continuously releasing new HBM products, including HBM5 and HBM5E chips starting in 2028.
results.summary: SK hynix announced 2025 plans to mass-produce the latest HBM3E/HBM4 stack technologies, including a 16-layer 48GB HBM3E with significantly improved training (18%) and inference (32%) performance, while continuing advanced packaging and hybrid bonding. The company aims for earlier HBM3E 16-layer shipments in H1 2025 and HBM4 12-layer in H2 2025, solidifying leadership with ongoing HBM pipeline (HBM5/5E from 2028) and a strategic TSMC collaboration for base-die logic processes.

results.title: SK Hynix, Samsung, Micron Compete for HBM4 Market Dominance - Businesskorea
results.author: Kim Eun-jin
highlights:
Following SK Hynix’s completion of next-generation HBM4 development and establishment of mass production systems last month, Samsung Electronics has also begun preparations for HBM4 mass production. Meanwhile, U.S.-based Micron recently announced that it has started shipping samples of its next-generation HBM4 memory, boasting record-breaking performance and efficiency.
[...]
Micron’s
[...]
1-gamma DRAM and proprietary CMOS-based die and packaging innovations.
[...]
SK Hynix, the leader in the HBM sector, announced that it had shipped 12-Hi HBM4 samples to major customers including NVIDIA in March, ahead of Micron and Samsung, and began preparations for mass production in September.
[...]
Samsung Electronics is also reported to have shipped HBM4 samples to customers including NVIDIA in September, with improved operating speeds of 11Gbps per second, matching Micron’s specifications. Samsung is also preparing for mass production of HBM4, aiming to start within this year.
results.summary:
Summary:
SK Hynix, Samsung, and Micron are racing to dominate the HBM4 market (~$100B). SK Hynix has advanced to mass production and shipped 12-Hi HBM4 samples; Micron is shipping 12-Hi HBM4 samples with over 2.8TB/s bandwidth and is pursuing customized HBM4E options for higher margins, while Samsung is preparing for HBM4 mass production and has shipped samples with 11Gbps speeds. The market catalyst is the imminent mass production and customer ramp installs (NVIDIA, AMD) driving competition and potential margin gains in HBM4/HBM4E.

results.title: HBM4 supply race intensifies as SK Hynix, Samsung speed up mass production
results.author: Daegeon Seok
highlights:
The variable is February. There is a time gap between initial production and high-volume manufacturing, when volumes are supplied to the market in earnest, and the turning point is February. Because initial volumes alone cannot meet demand from global big tech, February is when yields on production lines are raised to increase volumes. Samsung Electronics’ entry into the supply chain is expected to be decided at that point.
[...]
The key will be how quickly the high-volume production system can be stabilized after the second quarter, when HBM4 demand picks up in earnest. February will be a turning point for whether SK Hynix continues to dominate this year and whether Samsung Electronics secures a chance to turn the tables. An industry official said, “HBM4 has become a game where production speed is as important as technological capability.” The official added, “After February, who first establishes a stable high-volume production system will affect full-year results.”
results.summary:
Summary:
The HBM4 supply race is accelerating as Nvidia cyber-initiates Rubin mass production, with SK Hynix already securing early volumes and Samsung expected to decide to join by February, potentially easing supply constraints through a one-stop memory-packaging approach. Micron projects HBM4 full-scale production in 2H2026 and eyes a multi-billion-dollar TAM by 2028, underscoring a shifting, high-stakes AI memory market.

results.summary: NVIDIA’s HBM supply chain is expected to undergo a major reshuffle in 2026, with SK Hynix’s share of Nvidia’s HBM projected to fall from about 90% today to roughly 50%, as other suppliers (notably Samsung) ramp up to meet demand. This shift acts as the principal market catalyst for a more diversified HBM supply landscape in the near term.

toolCalls.name: exa_research
toolCalls.summary: The semiconductor supply chain for GPUs in 2025 faced significant bottlenecks driven by surging AI demand and capacity constraints at TSMC. As of early 2026, TSMC's 3nm capacity is fully allocated for the next 18-24 months, with lead times

input.query: GPU semiconductor supply chain 2025: TSMC capacity, CoWoS advanced packaging bottleneck, HBM3e supply, Nvidia AMD allocations

output.answer:
The semiconductor supply chain for GPUs in 2025 faced significant bottlenecks driven by surging AI demand and capacity constraints at TSMC. As of early 2026, TSMC's 3nm capacity is fully allocated for the next 18-24 months, with lead times exceeding 50 weeks, primarily due to high demand from AI applications ([Silicon Analysts, 01/22/2026](https://siliconanalysts.com/analysis/ai-chip-demand-surge-tsmc-3nm-supply-strained-lead-times-exceed-50-weeks)). The bottleneck has shifted from wafer supply to advanced packaging, specifically CoWoS (Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate), which remains a critical constraint. TSMC’s CoWoS capacity is heavily booked, with lead times surpassing 50 weeks, and NVIDIA holding an estimated 60-70% of this capacity, contributing to a significant allocation challenge for other customers ([Silicon Analysts, 03/13/2026](https://siliconanalysts.com/analysis/foundry-allocation-status-q1-2026)). 

In terms of advanced packaging, TSMC's CoWoS capacity is expanding, with plans to increase monthly throughput to around 120,000-130,000 wafers by the end of 2026, partly through outsourcing to OSAT partners like Amkor and SPIL. However, the demand for CoWoS remains intense, with NVIDIA alone reportedly booking more than half of TSMC's capacity, leaving little room for competitors ([Global Semi Research, 12/28/2025](https://globalsemiresearch.substack.com/p/tsmcs-cowos-capacity-scaling-up-outsourcing)). Additionally, HBM3e memory supply is critically tight, with prices expected to rise by 15-20% in the coming quarters, further complicating GPU production and availability ([Silicon Analysts, 01/22/2026](https://siliconanalysts.com/analysis/ai-chip-demand-surge-tsmc-3nm-supply-strained-lead-times-exceed-50-weeks)). Overall, the supply chain remains highly constrained, with capacity bottlenecks in both advanced packaging and memory components significantly impacting GPU availability in 2025 and early 2026.

# TSMC 3nm Lead Times Top 50 Weeks: AI Demand Strains GPU Supply

By Silicon Analysts

January 22, 2026

8 min read

[Foundry Economics](https://siliconanalysts.com/analysis/topic/foundry-economics) [Memory & HBM](https://siliconanalysts.com/analysis/topic/memory-hbm)

## Executive Summary

Surging AI demand, exemplified by rumored multi-billion dollar hyperscaler orders, is creating unprecedented bottlenecks in the advanced semiconductor supply chain. Lead times for 3nm-based accelerators are extending beyond 50 weeks, driven by fully allocated wafer capacity, a severe CoWoS packaging shortage, and tightening HBM3e supply, forcing a strategic shift towards long-term capacity planning.

1TSMC 3nm capacity is fully allocated for the next 18-24 months, with lead times now exceeding 50 weeks.

23nm wafer prices are firming around ~$20k, representing a ~1.8x cost increase over mature 7nm nodes.

3CoWoS packaging remains the primary bottleneck, with demand outstripping supply by an estimated 40-50%.

4HBM3e memory supply is critically tight, with prices expected to increase by 15-20% in the coming quarters.

At Silicon Analysts, we're tracking persistent rumors of a colossal, near ~$84 billion order for next-generation AI accelerators from a major hyperscaler, aimed at securing capacity through 2027. # Foundry Allocation Status Q1 2026: Where Capacity Is and Isn't

By Silicon Analysts

March 13, 2026

7 min read

[Supply Chain](https://siliconanalysts.com/analysis/topic/supply-chain) [Memory & HBM](https://siliconanalysts.com/analysis/topic/memory-hbm)

## Executive Summary

Of the 64 semiconductor fabs tracked in our Fab Explorer, 20 (31%) are constrained or worse as of Q1 2026. All six fully booked facilities are TSMC — three 2nm frontend fabs and three CoWoS advanced-packaging lines — with lead times stretching to 78-104 weeks. The bottleneck has shifted from wafer starts alone to a three-way constraint: advanced logic (2nm/3nm), CoWoS packaging, and HBM3E memory. Procurement teams that aren't already in the allocation queue for 2027 tape-outs face significant scheduling risk.

120 of 64 fabs constrained or worse — 6 fully booked, 6 severely constrained, and 8 constrained, representing 31% of all tracked facilities worldwide.

2All fully booked fabs are TSMC — Fab 20, Fab 22, and Fab 21 P3 (2nm/A16 frontend) plus AP3, AP5, and AP6 (CoWoS backend) are sold out through 2027.

3CoWoS is the binding constraint — All three TSMC advanced-packaging backend fabs are fully booked with 52-78 week lead times. NVIDIA holds an estimated 60-70% of total CoWoS capacity.

4HBM3E fully allocated — Both 8-hi and 12-hi HBM3E stacks are sold out for 2026, with prices rising 15-22% year-over-year.

citations.title: TSMC’s CoWoS Capacity: Scaling Up, Outsourcing, and Shifting to Next-Gen Packaging
citations.author: Global Semi Research
citations.text:
TSMC’s CoWoS Capacity: Scaling Up, Outsourcing, and Shifting to Next-Gen Packaging

# Global Semi Research

SubscribeSign in

# TSMC’s CoWoS Capacity: Scaling Up, Outsourcing, and Shifting to Next-Gen Packaging

Dec 28, 2025

11

1

Share

TSMC’s CoWoS advanced packaging capacity is currently a matter of intense interest for major technology companies worldwide. We’ve been closely tracking these developments, and the following insights are drawn from an expert transcript shared by an investor in the semiconductor space.

## TSMC’s CoWoS Capacity Targets for 2026

As of late 2025, TSMC’s monthly CoWoS capacity stands at approximately 75–80 KWPM. The company is targeting a significant ramp-up, aiming for 120–130 KWPM by the end of 2026 through optimizations in existing facilities like AP8 and adjustments at AP7.

Thanks for reading! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

Subscribe

Physical constraints, including limited land for new builds, mean TSMC is focusing on efficiency rather than entirely new dedicated CoWoS plants.

## Strategic Outsourcing in CoWoS Production

TSMC plans to outsource a portion of its CoWoS workload in 2026, with estimates of 240,000–270,000 wafers annually handed off to OSAT partners—primarily Amkor (180,000–190,000 wafers) and SPIL (60,000–80,000 wafers).

This shift is driven by multiple factors:

Margin Optimization: TSMC prioritizes high-margin processes like silicon interposer and front-end CoW, outsourcing lower-margin substrate assembly and testing (requiring at least 50% gross margins for in-house work).

Bottleneck Relief: Surging CoWoS-L demand has redirected internal capacity toward complex RDL interposers and silicon bridges, making outsourcing of CoWoS-S or substrate steps a logical move.

Long-Term Transition: With CoPoS set for mass production by late 2027, heavy investment in round-wafer CoWoS lines risks future idling.

Qualified OSATs like Amkor and SPIL, already certified for NVIDIA products and offering cost advantages, make outsourcing attractive.

## OSAT Margins in CoWoS

Margins vary by scope:

Substrate-only (OS): 35%–40% (standard flip-chip, lower value).

Full CoW + OS: 40%–45% (higher due to scarcity; e.g., B200 packaging ~$300–400/unit).

CoWoS-R (high-density RDL): Up to 50%.

## Key Client Demands Shaping the Market

### NVIDIA’s Leading Role

NVIDIA dominates, with 2025 CoW…

citations.title: Taiwan Semiconductor's CoWoS monthly production is expected to reach 127,000 pieces next year, with NVIDIA accounting for more than half, followed closely by Broadcom and AMD
citations.text:
# Taiwan Semiconductor's CoWoS monthly production is expected to reach 127,000 pieces next year, with NVIDIA accounting for more than half, followed closely by Broadcom and AMD

TSMC is accelerating the expansion of advanced packaging capacity to meet the explosive growth in demand for artificial intelligence chips.

On December 11, according to Taiwanese media reports, semiconductor equipment manufacturers revealed that TSMC, along with non-TSMC camps such as ASE Group, Amkor, and UMC, is accelerating the expansion of CoWoS capacity. By the end of 2026, TSMC's monthly capacity will reach 127,000 wafers, while the non-TSMC camp's capacity has surged from the original expectation of 26,000 wafers to 40,000 wafers, an increase of over 50%.

In terms of customer share distribution, NVIDIA continues to dominate the market, securing more than half of TSMC's CoWoS capacity, with annual bookings reaching 800,000 to 850,000 wafers. Following closely, Broadcom is expected to secure over 240,000 wafers of capacity by 2026, primarily supplying customers such as Meta and Google TPU. AMD ranks third, while MediaTek has officially entered the ASIC arena, booking nearly 20,000 wafers of capacity.

This capacity expansion plan aligns closely with Morgan Stanley's forecast from November. According to Wallstreetcn news, the firm had previously estimated that TSMC's CoWoS monthly capacity would reach 120,000 to 130,000 wafers, a significant upward revision of over 20% from the earlier estimate of 100,000 wafers. This move aims to match the newly added 3nm front-end wafer capacity to meet the enormous demand for AI chips.

It is worth noting that a previous article from Wallstreetcn stated that after NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang visited Taiwan seeking capacity for the next-generation "Rubin" platform, TSMC decided to increase the monthly 3nm front-end wafer capacity by 20,000 wafers, thereby driving a simultaneous increase in back-end advanced packaging capacity.

## GPU and ASIC Demand Surge Simultaneously

The capacity expansion decisions by TSMC and non-TSMC camps stem from demand from GPU and application-specific integrated circuit (ASIC) customers exceeding expectations.

Reports indicate that semiconductor equipment manufacturers have confirmed that despite the recent buzz around Google TPU and the emerging views of ASIC camps encroaching on the market, many industry play…

citations.title: Advanced packaging and HBM, not logic dies, were the bottlenecks on AI chip production in 2025 | Epoch AI
citations.text:
Advanced packaging and HBM, not logic dies, were the bottlenecks on AI chip production in 2025 | Epoch AI

Data Insight

Mar. 12, 2026

# Advanced packaging and HBM, not logic dies, were the bottlenecks on AI chip production in 2025

Cite

![Venkat Somala's avatar](https://epoch.ai/assets/images/team/square-small/venkat-somala.jpg)

By [Venkat Somala](https://epoch.ai/about/team/venkat-somala)

Frontier AI chips depend on three key inputs: advanced logic dies, high-bandwidth memory (HBM), and CoWoS advanced packaging (which integrates these components together). We estimate that the four largest AI chip designers collectively consumed around 90% of global CoWoS capacity and HBM supply in 2025, while consuming only 12% of advanced logic die production.

With AI chips absorbing nearly all available CoWoS and HBM supply in 2025, expanding production requires building new manufacturing facilities with long lead times. Logic fabrication was a softer constraint - the large majority of advanced-node capacity serves other markets, and AI’s share could grow by outbidding competing demand rather than waiting for new fabs. However, today’s newest AI chips increasingly use the 3nm process node, where they will likely take up a much [larger share of production and could run into capacity constraints](https://newsletter.semianalysis.com/p/the-great-ai-silicon-shortage).

Epoch's work is free to use, distribute, and reproduce provided the source and authors are credited under the Creative Commons BY license.

## Learn more about this graph

Frontier AI chips like NVIDIA’s H100 and B200 require three critical supply chain inputs: advanced logic dies fabricated at leading-edge process nodes, high-bandwidth memory (HBM) stacks, and CoWoS (Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate) advanced packaging to integrate these components. While all three inputs are necessary, they face very different supply-demand dynamics. See our [detailed methodology](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1JCkykRPEeT6SdRro2n3j0nrRCYUxmYh-MGo5ojRCK8Q/edit?usp=sharing) for full technical documentation.

We find that the four largest AI chip designers — NVIDIA, Google, AMD, and Amazon — collectively consumed over 90% of global CoWoS packaging capacity and HBM supply by value in 2025, but only about 12% of advanced logic die production. This asymmetry suggests that scaling AI chip production in 2025 was primarily b…

Advanced packaging is becoming one of the biggest constraints for the AI industry, and according to a new report, NVIDIA has already secured a giant share of the CoWoS capacity.

## NVIDIA Has Reserved More Than 50% of TSMC’s CoWoS Production, with Broadcom and AMD Following

We have covered specifics about advanced packaging quite a few times in the past week, but one of the more interesting information that has surfaced is that NVIDIA is projected to 'gobble up' more than half of TSMC's CoWoS capacity moving into 2026, which is likely to target the ongoing Blackwell Ultra rampup, as well as prepare for the next-generation Rubin architecture. A [report by DigiTimes](https://www.digitimes.com.tw/tech/dt/n/shwnws.asp?CnlID=1&Cat=40&id=0000740550_AU566GVP4Y57RS1NPB5SV) states that NVIDIA has booked 800,000 to 850,000 wafers for 2026, which represents a substantial allocation compared to what competitors like Broadcom and AMD have been allocated.

citations.title: AI Semiconductor Supply Chain: The Bottleneck Has Shifted
citations.author: Arjun Mehta
citations.text:
AI Semiconductor Supply Chain: The Bottleneck Has Shifted

[Next Waves Insight](https://nextwavesinsight.com/)

Analytical briefings for engineers, PMs, and CTOs

![](https://nextwavesinsight.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/hardware-featured-1.png)

# The AI Chip Shortage Never Ended. It Just Changed Shape.

April 26, 2026March 28, 2026

by

[Arjun Mehta](https://nextwavesinsight.com/author/arjun-mehta/)

4 min read

Key Takeaways

- [Contact](https://nextwavesinsight.com/contact/)
- The AI chip shortage didn’t end — it transformed. The bottleneck shifted from wafer supply to CoWoS advanced packaging, which [TSMC](https://nextwavesinsight.com/tsmc-advanced-packaging-nvidia-ai-supply-chain-2026/) has sold out through 2026.
- NVIDIA locked in over 70% of TSMC’s CoWoS-L capacity; the remaining allocation is split among AMD, Broadcom, Marvell, and others — creating a structural constraint that compounds with [HBM](https://nextwavesinsight.com/hbm-memory-bottleneck-ai-supply-chain-2026/) 3E supply tightness.
- Hyperscalers are bypassing the shortage with [custom silicon](https://nextwavesinsight.com/custom-silicon-google-apple-meta-microsoft-nvidia-2026/)(TPUs, Trainium, Maia), but this accelerates AI chip market fragmentation rather than resolving the underlying supply problem.

Key Claim: AI chip demand has outpaced supply consistently since 2022, but the shortage has shifted from foundry capacity to memory bandwidth.

The narrative that emerged in mid-2024 — that the AI chip shortage was easing — was accurate in the narrow sense. Wafer supply for AI accelerators had improved. It was wrong in the more important sense: the constraint had migrated downstream, from wafer fabrication to packaging, and from general GPU supply to a small number of highly specialised processes that the entire industry relies on.

The NextWave Signal — Sharp analysis, twice a week.

Two years into the AI infrastructure buildout, the supply chain for high-end AI silicon is not unconstrained. It has a different shape.

Table of Contents

Toggle

## The Packaging Chokepoint

The bottleneck that matters most in 2025 and 2026 is Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate (CoWoS) packaging — the process that allows High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) to be co-packaged with AI accelerators at the density that modern AI workloads require. Without CoWoS, a wafer is not a finished AI accelerator; it is a piece of s…

TSMC and the Advanced Packaging Surge Fueled by NVIDIA Corporation

![Dr. Robert Castellano's Semiconductor Deep Dive Newsletter](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!07Oo!,w_40,h_40,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc5d80641-ebc3-4eb6-9007-5dd3d3cd1d90_274x274.png)

# Dr. Robert Castellano's Semiconductor Deep Dive Newsletter

SubscribeSign in

# TSMC and the Advanced Packaging Surge Fueled by NVIDIA Corporation

![Dr. Robert Castellano's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!1f0t!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff6aebf1a-59ab-4dfc-b1b7-432495a787e6_1939x1939.png)

Dec 08, 2025

∙ Paid

5

Share

What’s in this Article:

Sections includedNVIDIA-Driven Demand SurgeTransition from CoWoS to CoPoSPackaging Capacity and Expansion PlansKLA’s Complementary Role in the Packaging ChainCombined Ecosystem and Supplier AlignmentFinancial and Investor OutlookGlobal Market Position and Competitive ShareChiayi Packaging Cluster ExpansionInvestor Takeaway

Tables includedTable 1. NVIDIA-Driven CoWoS Demand OutlookTable 2. Comparison of TSMC Advanced Packaging PlatformsTable 3. TSMC Advanced Packaging Capacity RoadmapTable 4. KLA Advanced Packaging Revenue OutlookTable 5. Representative Packaging Supply Chain IntegrationTable 6. Packaging Segment Financial Contribution (2024–2029 Projection)Table 7. Advanced Packaging Market Share by Supplier (2024–2029)Table 8. Chiayi Advanced Packaging Expansion Overview

### Introduction

The convergence of artificial intelligence, high-bandwidth memory (HBM), and chiplet architectures has made packaging the new fulcrum of semiconductor performance. TSMC (TSM) has emerged as the indispensable manufacturing cornerstone of this transition, while KLA Corporation (KLAC)—the industry’s metrology and inspection leader—has become its arbiter of quality. Together, they represent the two interlocking sides of an industry undergoing structural realignment: one builds the packages, the other assures their integrity.

For TSMC, NVIDIA’s (NVDA) next-generation GPU roadmap has created sustained pressure on its advanced-packaging capacity. CoWoS (Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate) output is running near full utilization, while the transition to CoPoS (Chip-on-Panel…

results.title: TSMC CoWoS capacity grows as Nvidia reserves supply — Silicon Report
highlights:
•Apr 9, 2
[...]
26•Updated
[...]
16d ago
[...]
TSMC says its CoWoS advanced packaging line is growing as it adds capacity, while Nvidia has reportedly reserved most of TSMC’s most advanced packaging supply. That shifts the planning question from only how many accelerators can be sourced to how many finished, multi-die packages can actually clear assembly and ship on schedule. TSMC North America packaging solutions head Paul Rousseau framed CoWoS as “the natural extension of Moore’s Law into the third dimension.”.. That warning fits the constraint TSMC is describing: if wafer output rises faster than packaging capacity, the limiting factor becomes the post-fab step that turns separate dies into a usable high-performance module. The tell is in the reservation behavior around Nvidia, which implies that access to packaging capacity may matter as much as access to leading-edge compute itself when systems are being scheduled.
results.summary: TSMC’s CoWoS advanced packaging capacity is expanding even as Nvidia reportedly reserves most of the top-tier supply, shifting planning from raw wafer supply to the ability to finish and ship multi-die packages. The situation highlights a potential bottleneck in post-fab assembly that could affect Nvidia, Intel, and other customers’ access to timely, high-end packaging.

results.summary: TSMC plans to boost CoWoS back-end packaging capacity to 127,000 wafers/month by end-2026, with NVIDIA securing over half the share and Broadcom, AMD also expanding; this aligns with strong AI-chip demand and Morgan Stanley’s forecast, signaling a rapid uplift in AI GPU/ASIC supply and capacity expansion across both TSMC and non-TSMC fabs.

results.title: TSMC Reserves 70% of 2025 CoWoS-L Capacity for NVIDIA | TechPowerUp
highlights: Rumors previously suggested that NVIDIA might scale back its CoWoS orders from TSMC. However, according to a report from Economic Daily News, orders for TSMC's advanced packaging have instead seen a surge. NVIDIA's Blackwell architecture GPUs are in strong demand, leading the company to secure over 70% of TSMC's CoWoS-L advanced packaging capacity for 2025. Shipment volumes are projected to rise by more than 20% each quarter, with total annual shipments expected to surpass 2 million units. Meanwhile, following the U.S. announcement of the Stargate project—which is anticipated to drive new AI server demand—NVIDIA is reportedly considering placing additional orders with TSMC. During TSMC's earnings call in January, Chairman C.C. Wei stated that the company is continuously expanding its advanced packaging capacity to keep pace with customer demand. According to reports, advanced packaging revenue accounted for roughly 8% in 2024 and is projected to exceed 10% in 2025.
results.summary: NVIDIA is securing over 70% of TSMC’s 2025 CoWoS-L advanced packaging capacity for Blackwell GPUs, signaling strong demand and potential shipments exceeding 2 million units annually as AI/server demand grows. TSMC is expanding CoWoS capacity and footprint, with plans for new facilities (some delayed) to support this surge.

As ASIC players hunt Nvidia, TSMC reaps the benefits
[...]
- Nvidia narrows CoWoP PCB partners to three amid advanced packaging crunch
[...]
- TSMC plans 12 fabs in Arizona as supply chain shifts from passive to active
[...]
at GTC 2026: SK Hynix, Samsung, and Micron battle for AI memory supremacy
results.summary: TSMC is expanding its CoWoS advanced packaging capacity, with Nvidia placing bookings that exceed half of the 2026–27 demand, signaling strong GPU/ASIC appetite and driving a broader push by TSMC and peers (ASE, Amkor, UMC) to add capacity amid rising packaging demand. This suggests Nvidia-driven demand is a key market catalyst for advancing these packaging expansions in the near term.

results.title: Breaking the Silicon Ceiling: TSMC Targets 33% CoWoS Growth to Fuel Nvidia’s Rubin Era
results.author:
By:
   TokenRing AI
highlights:
As 2025 draws to a close, the primary bottleneck in the global artificial intelligence race has shifted from the raw fabrication of silicon wafers to the intricate art of advanced packaging. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) (NYSE: TSM) has officially set its sights on a massive expansion for 2026, aiming to increase its CoWoS (Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate) capacity by at least 33%. This aggressive roadmap is a direct response to the insatiable demand for next-generation AI accelerators, particularly as Nvidia (NASDAQ: NVDA) prepares to transition from its Blackwell Ultra series to the revolutionary Rubin architecture.
[...]
Looking beyond 20
[...]
6, the
[...]
is expected to integrate
[...]
technology into its 3DFabric roadmap by 2027 to support even larger multi-die configurations.
results.summary: TSMC plans a 33% increase in CoWoS packaging capacity by 2026 to support Nvidia’s Rubin era, signaling a shift to System-on-Package/SoIC for high-performance AI chips and risking a capacity race among Nvidia, AMD, and CSPs.

input.query: US China export controls AI chips 2025 Nvidia H20 Blackwell

results.title: Trump says China, other countries can't have Nvidia's top AI chips | Reuters
results.author: Alexandra Alper
highlights:
chips for US companies
[...]
Nov 2 (Reuters) - Artificial intelligence giant Nvidia's (NVDA.O), opens new tab most advanced chips will be reserved for U.S. companies and kept out of China and other countries, U.S. President Donald Trump said.
[...]
During a taped interview that aired on Sunday on CBS' "60 Minutes" program and in comments to reporters aboard Air Force One, Trump said only U.S. customers should have access to the top-end Blackwell chips offered by Nvidia, the world's most valuable company by market capitalization.
[...]
Trump told CBS he would not allow the sale of the most advanced Blackwells to Chinese companies, but he did not rule out a path for them to obtain a less capable version of the chip. "We will let them deal with Nvidia but not in terms of the most advanced," he said during the "60 Minutes" interview.
[...]
Nvidia has not sought U.S. export licenses for the Chinese market because of Beijing's stance on the company, CEO Jensen Huang said last week.
results.summary: Trump says Nvidia’s most advanced Blackwell AI chips will be reserved for U.S. customers only, potentially restricting China and other countries from accessing the top-end hardware and signaling tighter export controls that could affect global AI chip supply and geopolitics. The move follows recent Nvidia supply to South Korea and ongoing debates over scaled-down versions for China.

results.title: Nvidia says U.S. will limit sales of advanced chips to China : NPR
results.author: Emily Feng
highlights:
Nvidia says the U.S. government is going ahead with export controls that will limit sales to China of one of its most advanced semiconductor chips used to power artificial intelligence system.
[...]
In a filing to the Securities and Exchange Commission on Tuesday, Nvidia said it expects the controls to cost them about $5.5 billion in charges due to the H20 chip inventory and prior sales now covered by the new restrictions and which they will not be able to sell.
[...]
The same day as NPR's story was published, Nvidia's disclosure says U.S. authorities told them there would be export controls on the H20, which require Nvidia to apply for licenses each time it wants to sell the chip to customers in China. This week, U.S. authorities also told the company the controls would be effective indefinitely.
[...]
Nvidia's stock sank by as much as
results.summary: Nvidia disclosed that U.S. export controls will restrict sales of its advanced H20 AI chip to China, a move tied to an expected $5.5 billion charge and a broader push to limit China-focused AI hardware. The development follows earlier reports that restrictions paused after Nvidia’s lobbying, and comes as the company threads a path toward U.S. manufacturing investments and potential stock impact.

results.title: US to allow Nvidia H200 chip shipments to China, Trump says | Reuters
results.author: Stephen Nellis
highlights:
WASHINGTON, Dec 8 (Reuters) - The United States will allow Nvidia's (NVDA.O), opens new tab H200 processors, its second-best artificial intelligence chips, to be exported to China and collect a 25% fee on such sales, U.S. President Donald Trump said on Monday.
[...]
He said the U.S. Commerce Department was finalizing details of the arrangement and the same approach would apply to other AI chip firms such as Advanced Micro Devices (AMD.O), opens new tab and Intel (INTC.O), opens new tab.
[...]
Trump's post said the fee to be paid to the U.S. government was "$25%", and a White House official confirmed he meant 25%, higher than the 15% proposed in August.
[...]
A White House official said that the 25% fee would be collected as an import tax from Taiwan, where the chips are made, to the United States, where the chips will undergo a security review by U.S. officials before being exported to China.
[...]
According to a report released on Sunday by the non-partisan think tank, the Institute
results.summary: Nvidia’s H200 chips will be allowed for export to China with a 25% U.S. import tax, a compromise that preserves some U.S. AI leadership and could unlock limited Chinese demand while safeguards keep national security in focus. Nvidia stock rose ~2% after-hours on the news, with similar honorably-structured potential implications for AMD and Intel.

results.title: U.S. Export Controls and China: Advanced Semiconductors
highlights:
controls could cede
[...]
- Expanded the FDPR to apply to SME and chips.
[...]
- Further restricted SME for16 PRC entities.
[...]
- Added China wide-controls for high-bandwidth memory (HBM), dynamic random-access memory (DRAM), and advanced packaging SME; end use/user controls for other SME.
[...]
- Exempted countries that align with U.S. policies.
[...]
- Added "Red-Flag" guidance that requires certain corporate due diligence.
[...]
- Added 140 PRC and PRC-tied entities to the EL and removed three firms from the VEU.
[...]
- Added due diligence rules for exporters.
[...]
- Proposed the AI Diffusion Rule which would create a global licensing system for advanced chips, computers, and advanced closed-weight dual-use AI model weights:
[...]
- Data center VEUs: subject to a presumption of approval.
[...]
- Added 42 PRC entities to the EL.
[...]
- Rescinded the January 2025 AI Diffusion Rule.
- Issued guidance that any use of Huawei's Ascend AI chips violates U.S. export controls.
results.summary:
Summary:
The report highlights that U.S. export controls on advanced semiconductors target China’s access to cutting-edge chips and related AI/compute capabilities, aiming to curb China’s indigenous semiconductor development while maintaining U.S. leadership. It notes ongoing policy actions since 2018, associated gaps/workarounds, and key issues for Congress on tightening controls and managing global supply-chain implications.

results.title: The Nvidia H200 export saga, as it happened — Beijing ponders response and buyers line up, while Blackwell remains locked behind restrictions | Tom's Hardware
results.author:
News Analysis 
 
 
By
 Luke James   
 
published
 
10 December 2025
highlights:
Chinese regulators have not publicly responded to the U.S. announcement but are said to have begun internal reviews. According to reporting by *The Information*, officials from the National Development and Reform Commission and the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology have held meetings with Alibaba, ByteDance, and Tencent to assess their projected demand for H200 GPUs. The companies have been asked to provide use cases and expected unit volumes, with the understanding that the government may impose caps or usage guidelines.
[...]
Ultimately, Nvidia is unlikely to resume large-scale H200 production unless it receives strong approval from both U.S. and Chinese regulators, and equally strong demand from Chinese customers. The chip is already considered a transitional product within Nvidia’s roadmap, with Blackwell occupying the top end of the current generation and Rubin expected to extend that lead in 2026.
[...]
The approval of H200 exports arguably represents
results.summary:
Summary:
Nvidia’s H200 export restrictions and China-related licensing delays are creating a bottleneck at the gate for Asian buyers, with Beijing weighing potential responses while customers line up for shipments once approvals clear. The situation keeps Blackwell-era restrictions in place, signaling continued regulatory risk and delayed AI GPU deployments for Chinese firms.

results.title: H20 Nvidia Chip Controls May Backfire on Washington
results.author: Ray Wang
highlights:
On April 15, U.S. chipmaker Nvidia published a filing to the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission indicating that the government has restricted the company from selling its less advanced graphics processing unit (GPU)—the H20—to China. The company is now required to obtain a license from the U.S. Commerce Department’s Bureau of Industry and Security to sell the H20 and any other chips “achieving the H20’s memory bandwidth, interconnect bandwidth, or combination thereof” to China, according to the filing.
[...]
The new restriction came roughly a week after NPR reported that the Trump administration had decided to back off on regulating the H20. Prior to that report, curbs on the H20 and chips with comparable performance had been widely anticipated by analysts on Wall Street, industry experts in Silicon Valley, and policy circles in Washington.
[...]
acquired prior to
[...]
,000 units of the H100, 270,000 of the H20, and tens of thousands of cards such as the L20 and L40.
results.summary: Market-relevant headline: U.S. export controls are tightening on AI chips, with Nvidia’s H20 and similar chips (AMD MI308X, Intel Gaudi) now requiring licenses to China, potentially restricting sales to the Chinese market. Catalyst: the new thresholds expand to cover less advanced chips and could limit multiple high-performance GPUs beyond the already restricted H20, signaling a broader shift in tech export controls.

results.author: Charlotte Trueman
highlights:
July 15, 2025ByCharlotte TruemanHave your say
[...]
Nvidia hopes to "soon" begin shipping its H20 chips to China, having received assurances from the US government that it will be granted the necessary licenses.
[...]
In ablog postannouncing the update, the chipmaker also revealed it has developed a new “fully compliant” chip for the Chinese market, the Nvidia RTX PRO GPU, which the company says is “ideal for digital twin AI for smart factories and logistics.”
[...]
The granting of export licensesreverses a decisionmade by the Trump Administration in April to impose additional restrictions on exports of H20 chips to China, which, at the time, Nvidia said would cost it approximately $5.5 billion of charges associated with inventory, purchase commitments, and related reserves.
[...]
However, speaking to reporters on a visit to Beijing this week, Huang also cautioned that stopping US companies from selling to China risks handing AI leadership to domestic companies like Huawei.
results.summary: US grants Nvidia export licenses to ship H20 AI GPUs to China, reversing Trump-era restrictions; Nvidia also unveils a new RTX PRO GPU inspired by Blackwell, positioned for digital twin AI in smart factories and logistics. This signals renewed market access for Nvidia in China and a potential boost to its AI hardware ecosystem, while highlighting policy-driven caution from Huang about US-China competition.

results.title: Trump greenlights exports of Nvidia H200 chips to China | CNN Business
results.author: Elisabeth Buchwald
highlights:
President Donald Trump on Monday announced he’s removing export controls on Nvidia’s H200 chips to China, a key reversal in what’s become part of a broader trade war between the world’s two biggest economies.
[...]
“I have informed President Xi, of China, that the United States will allow NVIDIA to ship its H200 products to approved customers in China, and other Countries, under conditions that allow for continued strong National Security. President Xi responded positively!” Trump wrote in the post.
[...]
Trump also said the Department of Commerce is finalizing a similar approach for AMD, Intel and other American chip companies.
[...]
The decision comes after Trump has struck arrangements with chipmakers earlier this year, including an agreement with Nvidia and AMD to pay the US government 15% of revenues from chip sales to China. Trump also announced in August that the government would take a 10% stake in Intel.
results.summary:
Summary:
Trump greenlit the export of Nvidia’s H200 AI chips to China with conditions to safeguard national security, signaling a major relaxation of U.S. export controls that could boost American chipmakers’ competitiveness while tying revenue flows to US oversight. The move excludes higher-end Blackwell and Rubin chips and hints at a broader framework for other US chipmakers, potentially reshaping AI hardware supply dynamics and Sino-American tech competition.
